item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is 7 years old but it's so rare to find someone else with prosopagnosia!

Unfortunately the assault thing did happen to me and I did have massive trouble identifying him…

+1 for the article btw. Seems her perception thing let her phase through people's skin too?

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Looks like you duplicated your comment from the last page. As a general note, if you find that a comment is taking a long time to post, try opening up the same discussion page and refreshing it before repeatedly submitting the post. Sometimes hitting the "post it" button multiple times results in multiples of the same comment being generated.

item.title: damn,,,
item.content:encoded: I got here from Reddit and really enjoyed this one! the part where she was talking about how nice people's bones are was honestly kinda creepy and the twist was unexpected +1 for sure

item.title: damn,,,
item.content:encoded: I got here from Reddit and really enjoyed this one! the part where she was talking about how nice people's bones are was honestly kinda creepy and the twist was unexpected +1 for sure

item.title: -1 because implys that there is something spookyer than a skeleton witch is objectively false false
item.content:encoded: raw text

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are several aspects of this that I like. I like how the girl suddenly has this fascination and perhaps attraction with bones and skeletons, I like the twist at the end, and I like the implications that come with it. While I view these aspects as sort of disconnected, that only makes it creepier. Why specifically is Dr. Wright interested in the bone anomaly? Are there more like Dr. Wright? +1 easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…did she just develop a fetish for bones?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eesh! That twist had me squirming. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it because it's a rational extension of the question "what would scare a person that saw everyone as a skeleton?". The answer, of course, is "someone who didn't look like a skeleton".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I concur. The dialogue was (IMHO) done well, it was one of the reasons it got plus from me. The second was that twist. Again, nicely handled I thought.

Dialogue like that where there is a subtext - that is to say both characters out of their respective comfort zones and not relaxing into their new positions at all is a pretty abstract to get though with just dialogue.
It's very easy to forget that way over 90% of communication is not just simply words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i like it and gave it an upvote but one thing i don't like is this part " by the lead researcher of the anomaly, Dr. Miles Wright Dr. Adelaide Fredricks." it sort of spoils the interview logs because you will know that something happened that caused him to be replaced.

item.title: Something incredible
item.content:encoded:
I've just realized something. What Saffron is experiencing is basically a high-level sensory filter. It's perceptual fuckery at an anomalous level. And how was it defeated? With another perceptual fuckery being imposed on her. A simpler, stronger one. In addition to "see everything vertebrate as converted, per the following algorithm", it was "see Xyrdujniqf as Standard Human Disguise #1247".

That's not a randomly-selected number, by the way.

Xyrdujniqf is extremely lucky that Saffron never saw any of the media which would have allowed her to recognize the disguise as a disguise.

"Dr Miles Wright" looks exactly like Shia Labeouf.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seeing that folks in the comments have complained that the dialogue - particularly Doc Wright's - seems unnatural and stilted kinda confuses me. To me they sound exactly like a teenager trying to sound tough and streetwise to an authority figure and an adult who is trying to sound all hip and with it to gain a teenager's trust. Of course they're going to sound like a couple of awkward fakers.

Anywho, +1 for dat twist, tho'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For these reasons, I would assume that non-photographic art doesn't count.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was my first thought, too, and I'm a bit disappointed that it was never addressed. In fact, it wasn't even addressed what the consequences of it not interacting with anything but someone's skeleton is.
Does its hand just phase through, doing absolutely no damage? Or does it pierce through flesh, seriously damaging it, despite meeting no resistance?
How does 1487 see itself? The description doesn't specifically state that it only applies to other organisms, but I still feel like that would be something researchers would inquire about.

The entity states that its condition applies to pictures and photos as well, but what exactly does that encompass? Does that also apply to drawings? Sketches? Stick figures?
If it observed someone drawing an organism (say, a dog), would 1487 see the dog? Or only its skeleton? At what point of dog-completion does the image, from 1487's point of view, turn from an indistinct drawing into a skeleton of a dog?
Or would it always see the drawing-in-progress as being one of a skeleton, from the very beginning? This would imply some sort of predictive ability which is huge. What about extremely photorealistic, but not photographic, art? What if 1487 itself was asked to draw a dog?

If drawings or other non-photographic art doesn't count for the purposes of the anomalous condition, what about photographs that have been edited in some way? Digital editing, drawing over the photographs, photo collages, do these still count as photos and are thus subject to the condition? Or do the depicted organisms stop being seen as skeletons as soon as the images are edited?

Also, if one showed 1487 a photograph or a recording of somewhere it's never been to with some organism obscuring the background, would it be able to accurately describe the background that would not be obscured by the skeleton? If so, that could be a useful ability that I'm sure the Foundation would take note of.
If non-photographic art does count and one showed it a fairly photorealistic image (with background not based on a real location and an organism covering a part of it), what would 1487 describe the background as? This bearing in mind that that background has never existed, neither in the real world nor in the image. What if the image is based on a real location? What if the artist painted something completely ridiculous in the background before covering it up with the organism in question?

I just came up with these ideas after a few minutes of brainstorming. I'm sure the Foundation would think of these as well, given 1487's cooperation and its potential usefulness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is a perfect way to end the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Instant favourite. Damn, I can't believe I missed this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not an issue of danger, it's about difficulty of containment. People aren't safe because you can't just crate them up, stick them in a giant warehouse and forget about them. They need food, water and various types of maintenance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Euclid classification is generally given to anything sentient and/or sapient, just because intelligence can result in unpredictable behavior. Maybe she finds a way to escape, or she suddenly becomes manic or violent.

Furthermore, she's not Keter as she doesn't pose an immediate threat to humanity as a whole, even of she was hostile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does the pass-through-tissue effect extend to indirect contact? If, for example, 1487 tried to reach into your lower abdomen while wearing gloves, would they become intangible with her? Collide with your skin and prevent 1487's hand from going any farther? Carve a path through your soft tissue without 1487 meeting any resistance? Or would 1487's hands pass through the gloves as well? (Similar issues could arise from people's clothing.)

I feel like this detail would be important in determining how potentially dangerous 1487's abilities are, and perhaps whether her Euclid classification is warranted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1487 is unable to perceive or interact with any parts of living organisms other than the internal skeletal system.

Important bit bolded. I don't think that one's epidermis and hair constitutes a separate dead organism. Especially when its still connected to the body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then why can't she see epidermis or hair?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Have I said that the story in this is fucking awesome yet?

Because it is. It really is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1487 is unable to perceive or interact with any parts of living organisms other than the internal skeletal system. Due to this, the entity is able to identify different animal species, but tends to have difficulty in distinguishing between individual members. Nonliving materials, as well as organisms that possess external skeletal systems or otherwise lack internal skeletal structure, are not subject to this effect.

In short, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can she eat meat?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddamn, how did I miss this the first go-'round? This is great!

While I like that the Foundation is ever-pragmatic, the last part of the addendum is blech. Still, an enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea and the execution, except for how little you used the cold tone of the foundation, especially in the footnote. It kind of made sense in the footnote, but in other places it was just.. Meh. +0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Miles Wright" reminds me alot of horrible Ace Attorney fanfiction.

Yeah… I really need to get less lazy with names…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that was unexpectedly chilling. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had a similar canon, that she saw him because he named her and thus formed some kind of a familiarity bond.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, now I like this even more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I thought 1487 suddenly became Chris Tucker at the beginning and needed to check the description again.

Aside from that, I like the overall mystery and thinking why she was so afraid of him. +1 for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Miles Wright" reminds me alot of horrible Ace Attorney fanfiction.

Bits and babbles aside, the twist was great.

Have this +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a really, really cool list you have there, but unfortunately I, as the author, am wholly unconvinced that this skip is anything other than Euclid based on my understanding of the classification, so you're argument of "but there's things that are alive that are Safe" ain't cutting it. I always use this rule of thumb in my articles, and it's not like there aren't highly rated articles that are Euclid because they are alive.

Regardless, as the author of this skip, I'm now kindly requesting that this line of discussion be stopped due to the fact that a) it's not truly changing anyone's opinion, b) it seems highly unlikely that anyone will budge from their position here, c) it's really not that important, and d), we're clogging up the Recent Posts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Happen" does not mean "literally any event occurs in the box." "Happen" generally refers to bad things.

Now she can move through people, what if she can eventually move through steel?

This kind of "what if" thing can be applied to every SCP evenly. Currently there is absolutely zero reason to suspect that this is possible.

Disregarding all of that, multiple highly rated articles feature living moving Safe class objects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"If you lock it in a box, and nothing will happen, it's safe." Some passive object that just sits.

"If you lock it in a box, and you don't know what will happen, it's Euclid." Something that may try to unsuccessfully escape, it may change state (say when it gets emotionally distressed or dies). This describes humans in general. You have to feed them and actively care for them, and even then, you're not sure how things will evolve. Now she can move through people, what if she can eventually move through steel? What happens when she dies or gets in a bad mood?

"If you put it in a box and something bad happens anyway, it's Keter" we know what this one is for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess the locked box test has lost popularity lately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Things that are alive are automatically Euclid in my mind because the fact that is alive and capable of movement and needs special care as such necessarily qualifies as Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I checked, there are about 40 more pages of safes (although it gets really fuzzy the further back you go). And yeah, it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I said, usually. Anyways, our Safe/Euclid/Keter classification system is already impractical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/search:site/q/safe%20humanoid

185 pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All sentient organisms are usually considered Euclid, since they are unpredictable thinking beings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is she Euclid? Unless Humanoid Containment Chamber #3821 is just a bunch of cows standing on top of one another, it should be able to contain her indefinitely on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I think his dialogue is supposed to be forced--he's a fake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Wright dialogue isn't very good. Feels forced. You should fix it. Her dialogue is a leetle forced at times too.

Beyond that, though, the twist really made this for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I prefer my original interpretation of why she doesn't like Wright now.

She kept seeing him in the flesh because he was the only person that was consistently personable with her. Everyone else at the Foundation would be treating her with clinical detachment, while he used her real name and shot the breeze on occasion. He was the only one that saw her as a person, and hence vice versa. The only problem is that she is remarkably well adjusted to her circumstances. She's so acclimatized to seeing clean bone that all that gross skin and flesh is just creepy. About how a normal young girl would feel when encountering a skeleton.

Wright hit the bricks because he knows how the Foundation is, and is not interested in spending the rest of his life in a containment cell being poked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't do anything for me. The tone of the interviews was so bad that I downvoted on reflex. I continued reading, because I figured there had to be some twist that made up for it. I thought the shift of focus from the SCP to Dr. Wright was original, but not particularly interesting. All we know is that he's probably not human, and that just isn't enough for me to care. I wanted to go back and upvote or neutral vote, but the early interview tone is still cringey, regardless of how it changes in the end. Obviously enough other people like it that it doesn't matter, but I wouldn't be inclined to upvote unless this was significantly re-written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from the kind of awkward dialogue, I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel the interview logs are far too casual in description and characters.

Otherwise I can't say I'm specifically emotionally touched as I should've been for reasons I can't really understand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More input: The overly informal tone works very well for this article, as it stands to contrast the skip's overly-talkativeness and familiarity with her later silence. I didn't know what to expect when I read that… he was going to die? He raped her? Or was she just so angry that he was the only person left that she would be able to see? Or did she realize something more sinister… that he's not human? The addendum confirms the last.

If I might make one small suggestion, I might make it that it took her longer than a week to realize that something was wrong. If he's the only real person she's interacting with over and over again, it might be for some other reason that she continues to see him as human, and might not dawn on her that somethings wrong for a week. Basically, I might change the wording from "Well, over the course of the last few weeks" to "Well, sometime last month" which indicates that she's had plenty of time to realize exactly whats going on, and at some point she realized that he's just not human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I initially interpreted the part where the girl says that she "can still see Dr. Wright. All of him. No one else" as "she saw all of Dr. Wright, even the lovecraftian bits"

Almost right, right ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hit the nail on the head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m gonna call you Miley. Like the shitty singer.

This made me scroll back up and upvote. And then I got to the WTF at the end and I tried to upvote again. Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oooooooooooohhhhh. I giddit now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She can put her hand in someone's torso because the ribcage leaves an open space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is neat, but why is she an SCP? Like SCP-1247, the anomalous effect is not readily apparent.

item.title: **Staff Post**
item.content:encoded:
I do what I can.

No.

That's not the way you respond to someone who you just caused emotional duress, intentionally or no. You are, of course, perfectly free to express your opinion regarding the article and your thoughts on it, but you are not allowed to flippantly respond to people you made uncomfortable like that. Do not let this sort of behavior repeat itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had to read it a second time to get this; but, it's so obvious in retrospect that I have to conclude it's due to me being dense. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that escalated quickly. More or less echoing what everyone else has said, that was a neat twist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do what I can.

Rape is not a subject that's much tolerated around here; virtually every article that tries to use it to any effect ends up, at best, crass and tasteless, and more often flatly offensive. Even ignoring that, this girl's whole schtick is her inability to interact with human flesh, so why exactly was this your first expectation?

Because the 'doctor' comes off extremely creepily, and her sudden change in demeanor reminded me of a rape victim's trauma.
